patterns.name: Hidden Singular Subjects
patterns.what: Sometimes gets tricked by plural words hiding next to a singular subject, causing him to choose a plural verb.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Adriel saw 'cousins' and picked the plural 'are organising', missing that the main subject is the singular 'sister'.
He treated 'luggage' as a plural word, but it is actually an uncountable noun that needs a singular verb.
He picked the plural 'those' for shoes, forgetting that the word 'pair' makes it a singular item.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see these tricky subjects, use these strategies:
- **'as well as'** or **'together with'** → put brackets around that part and ignore it, matching your verb to the very first person mentioned.
- **'luggage'**, **'furniture'**, or **'information'** → these are uncountable nouns, so always treat them as singular.

patterns.name: Fixed Word Partners
patterns.what: Occasionally mixes up words that always go together, like specific prepositions, phrasal verbs, or paired conjunctions.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Adriel chose 'adaptable' instead of 'accustomed', missing the clue that it must pair with the preposition 'to'.
He paired 'No sooner' with 'when', but it is a time phrase that always needs to be paired with 'than'.
He wrote 'less' instead of 'example' to complete the common phrase 'For example'.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you learn a new word, always learn its partner! Treat these pairs as a single unit:
- **'congratulate'** always pairs with **'on'**
- **'accustomed'** always pairs with **'to'**
- **'No sooner'** always pairs with **'than'**

patterns.name: Missing Sentence Clues
patterns.what: Tends to pick a word that sounds okay on its own but doesn't quite fit the specific clues or contrast words hidden in the sentence.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Adriel chose 'reaps' for the garden, but a garden 'yields' (produces) crops, while a farmer 'reaps' (harvests) them.
He wrote 'used' instead of 'hard', missing the contrast clue 'Although it sounds simple' earlier in the sentence.
He wrote 'In conclusion' to introduce a new point, instead of using 'In fact' to add to the previous sentence.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see contrast words like **'Although'** or **'However'**, treat them as stop signs—they tell you the next part of the sentence will be the opposite of what you expect. Always read the sentence before and the sentence after the blank to make sure your word fits the whole story, not just the blank.
